seoTitle: How to Add DNS Records in Cloudflare (2026)
metaDescription: Add A, CNAME, MX, and TXT records in Cloudflare's DNS panel, set proxy status correctly, and confirm your domain mappings are live.
intro: Whether you're pointing a site to a new host or setting up mail routing, adding DNS records in Cloudflare follows the same core process. This guide covers building A, CNAME, MX, and TXT records from the DNS management panel and checking they've propagated correctly.

steps.heading: Open the DNS Records grid
steps.body: Select your domain in Cloudflare and open the DNS Records page from the left sidebar.

steps.heading: Click Add Record
steps.body: Click the '+ Add record' button to open a fresh row for configuring a new entry.

steps.heading: Choose your record type
steps.body: Use the Type dropdown to select A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, or TXT depending on what you're setting up.

steps.heading: Fill in the record details
steps.body: Enter the name, target value, and priority (for MX records) as needed for your chosen record type.

steps.heading: Set proxy status and TTL, then save
steps.body: Decide whether to proxy the record through Cloudflare, set an appropriate TTL, and save to deploy it live.

faq.question: What's the quickest way to point my domain to a new hosting IP?
faq.answer: Edit or add an A record with your root symbol as the name and your new server's IP address as the value.

faq.question: Should mail records like MX be proxied?
faq.answer: No, mail-related records should stay set to 'DNS Only' since Cloudflare's proxy is designed for web traffic, not email routing.

faq.question: How do I check if my new DNS record has propagated?
faq.answer: You can review the record status in Cloudflare's dashboard or use an external DNS propagation checker to see it resolving globally.

faq.question: What TTL should I use for a new record?
faq.answer: Leaving TTL on Auto is usually fine for most setups, as Cloudflare manages an appropriate value automatically for proxied records.
